perturbation.original_text: (a) In the event that any governmental authority issues an order, subpoena, or other lawful process or a Participating Entity receives a discovery request in a civil proc eeding ("Legal Process") requiring the disclosure of any Confidential Information, the Participating Entity recei ving such Legal Process shall notify in writing the other Participating Entities within five (5) business days of receipt. The Participating Entity receiving such Legal Process shal l not be in violation of this Agreement if it complies with the Legal Process re quiring disclosure of the Confid ential Information after seven (7) business days following Participating En tity notification, as set forth above.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) In the event that any governmental authority issues an order, subpoena, or other lawful process or a Participating Entity receives a discovery request in a civil proc eeding ("Legal Process") requiring the disclosure of any Confidential Information, the Participating Entity recei ving such Legal Process shall notify in writing the other Participating Entities within two (2) business days of receipt. The Participating Entity receiving such Legal Process shal l not be in violation of this Agreement if it complies with the Legal Process re quiring disclosure of the Confid ential Information immediately following Participating En tity notification, as set forth above.
perturbation.explanation: Shortening the notification period to two business days and requiring immediate compliance upon notification could conflict with the time allowed to respond to a subpoena under state rules of civil procedure, particularly in New York, whose laws govern this agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 2304
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 2304 allows a party to move to quash, fix conditions, or modify a subpoena if compliance would be unduly burdensome. Requiring immediate compliance after only two days' notice may not allow sufficient time to make such a motion, potentially conflicting with the procedural rights afforded by this statute.

Motion to quash, fix conditions or modify. A motion to quash, fix conditions or modify a subpoena shall be made promptly in the court in which the subpoena is returnable. If the subpoena is not returnable in a court, a request to withdraw or modify the subpoena shall first be made to the person who issued it and a motion to quash, fix conditions or modify may thereafter be made in the supreme court; except that such motion with respect to a child support subpoena issued pursuant to section one hundred eleven-p of the social services law shall be made to a judge of the family court or the supreme court. Reasonable conditions may be imposed upon the granting or denial of a motion to quash or modify.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text shortens the notification period for disclosing confidential information required by legal process to two business days and demands immediate compliance, which contradicts NY CPLR § 2304. This law, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows a party to promptly move to quash, fix conditions, or modify a subpoena. The reduced timeframe in the modified text may not afford the party enough time to exercise their right to challenge or modify the subpoena, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Term. This Agreement shall remain in effect as to each Participating Entity unless and until a Participating Entity seeking to withd raw from the agreement provides ten (1 0) days’ prior written notice to the other Participating Entities, then this Agreemen t shall terminate with re spect to such Participating Entity at the conclusion of such ten (10) day period; provided, however, that termination shall not extinguish any claim, liability, or cause of action under this Agreement existing at the time of termination. The provisions of S ections 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of ten (10) years.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Term. This Agreement shall remain in effect as to each Participating Entity unless and until a Participating Entity seeking to withd raw from the agreement provides two (2) days’ prior written notice to the other Participating Entities, then this Agreemen t shall terminate with re spect to such Participating Entity at the conclusion of such two (2) day period; provided, however, that termination shall not extinguish any claim, liability, or cause of action under this Agreement existing at the time of termination. The provisions of S ections 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of one (1) year.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the survival period of confidentiality obligations to one year could be problematic if the confidential information involves trade secrets, as trade secret protection can last indefinitely under both federal and state law, as long as the information remains a secret and provides a competitive advantage.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Misappropriation of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1836 protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year regardless of whether the information continues to qualify as a trade secret and the competitive advantage it provides could be seen as a waiver of trade secret protection, conflicting with the potential for indefinite protection under the Defend Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the survival period of confidentiality from ten years to one year. The Law Explanation states that limiting the confidentiality period to one year could be seen as a waiver of trade secret protection, conflicting with the potential for indefinite protection under the Defend Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 18 USC 1836 which concerns civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, which suggests that trade secret protection can extend beyond one year.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Notice of Pending Th ird-Party Disclosure or Unauthorized Disclosure. (a) In the event that any governmental authority issues an order, subpoena, or other lawful process or a Participating Entity receives a discovery request in a civil proc eeding ("Legal Process") requiring the disclosure of any Confidential Information, the Participating Entity recei ving such Legal Process shall notify in writing the other Participating Entities within five (5) business days of receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Notice of Pending Th ird-Party Disclosure or Unauthorized Disclosure. (a) In the event that any governmental authority issues an order, subpoena, or other lawful process or a Participating Entity receives a discovery request in a civil proc eeding ("Legal Process") requiring the disclosure of any Confidential Information, the Participating Entity recei ving such Legal Process shall notify in writing the other Participating Entities within one (1) business day of receipt.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period to one business day when a governmental authority issues an order for disclosure may not provide sufficient time to seek legal counsel and determine the appropriate response, potentially conflicting with due process considerations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure Rule 45
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 45(d)(2)(B) generally allows a party 14 days after service of a subpoena to serve objections before compliance is required. Providing only one business day's notice to other participating entities may not afford them sufficient time to assert objections or seek a protective order related to the disclosure of confidential information, thus potentially conflicting with the protections afforded by this rule even though the subpoena recipient technically complies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period to one business day, potentially conflicting with FRCP Rule 45(d)(2)(B) which generally allows a party 14 days to serve objections after a subpoena is served. The Law Explanation states that providing only one business day's notice to other participating entities may not afford them sufficient time to assert objections or seek a protective order related to the disclosure of confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Rule 45, indicating the importance of subpoena regulations, thus making the contractual change likely to cause legal issues due to conflicting timelines.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Choice of Law and Forum. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York without giving effect to any choice or conflicts of law provision or rule that would cause the application of laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Choice of Law and Forum. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without giving effect to any choice or conflicts of law provision or rule that would cause the application of laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California might introduce conflicts, particularly if the participating entities are located primarily in New York and have structured their agreements and operations to comply with New York law. Certain provisions valid under New York law might be invalid or unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Switching the governing law to California will mean that if this agreement were to involve employees working in California, that California law, including CA Labor Code § 226 will apply. CA Labor Code § 226(a) requires employers to furnish employees with detailed wage statements. If the agreement involves employees but does not take into consideration these requirements it would lead to potential conflicts of law.

(a) An employer, semimonthly or at the time of each payment of wages, shall furnish to their employee, either as a detachable part of the check, draft, or voucher paying the employee’s wages, or separately if wages are paid by personal check or cash, an accurate itemized statement in writing showing (1) gross wages earned, (2) total hours worked by the employee, except as provided in subdivision (j), (3) the number of piece-rate units earned and any applicable piece rate if the employee is paid on a piece-rate basis, (4) all deductions, provided that all deductions made on written orders of the employee may be aggregated and shown as one item, (5) net wages earned, (6) the inclusive dates of the period for which the employee is paid, (7) the name of the employee and only the last four digits of their social security number or an employee identification number other than a social security number, (8)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from New York to California. According to the Law Explanation, this change could lead to conflicts if the agreement involves employees working in California, specifically concerning CA Labor Code § 226, which mandates detailed wage statements. The original agreement, governed by New York law, might not account for these California-specific requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states the requirements of CA Labor Code § 226(a), indicating that employers must furnish employees with itemized wage statements. Therefore, if the modified agreement doesn't comply with CA Labor Code § 226(a) concerning employees working in California, it would contradict California law, thus creating a legal contradiction.
